headline: CORN REVERSES 7% DROP WITH 6.8% OVERNIGHT RUN
subheadline: Yesterday's hard break fully reversed before the open; the Belt's weather picture splits north from south.
lead: Corn ran $0.28 overnight to close at $4.41, erasing almost the entire 7% drop from Monday in a single session. That's not a bounce. That's a market telling you Monday's break was fund liquidation looking for a floor, not a fundamental shift. The 2026 planting season, behind the average through May before catching up between rain systems, is now showing cracks: Minnesota corn conditions slipped a point to 79% good-to-excellent this week while northern Illinois farmers are calling their stands excellent. Two different Belts, one price. Thursday's export sales will tell you which one the market is actually pricing.
the_takeaway: Corn's full reversal says Monday was fund liquidation, not a structural break. Thursday decides.
teaser: Corn erases Monday's 7% drop overnight. Minnesota conditions slipping. Farm bill draft drops today.

one_number.unit: U.S. cattle inventory impacted by drought
one_number.context: Beef Magazine's cattle market update puts 53% of the U.S. cattle inventory in drought-affected areas. That's not a footnote, that's the structural floor under live cattle prices. When the herd is already tight and drought is shrinking grazing resources across more than half the country's inventory, the processing constraints from the Cargill Fort Morgan lockout compound on top of a supply problem that started before any labor dispute.

yesterdays_call.summary: Producers with old-crop bushels in storage were told Monday's break was a window-closing move and to evaluate if basis was still firm.
yesterdays_call.note: Corn reversed 6.8% overnight. The window didn't close. It reopened. Reassess with fresh eyes Thursday after export sales.

sections.title: CORN'S FULL REVERSAL
sections.body: Corn closed $4.41, up $0.28¼ on the session. That is a 6.8% move in a single overnight run, the biggest single-day advance in months, and it came with no USDA report, no weather emergency, no crop tour. What it came with was a split crop condition picture: Minnesota corn slipped to 79% good-to-excellent this week while northern Illinois farmers are reporting stands as strong as they've seen. The funds who liquidated Monday found the floor they were looking for and stepped back in. **$4.41 is now the question**, not the answer. If the Belt's condition divergence widens and Thursday export sales come in above 400K MT, the overnight run has legs. If Minnesota's numbers keep sliding and the export desk is quiet, this is a dead-cat bounce that traps the buyers who just chased it.
sections.catalyst: Minnesota corn conditions slipped to 79% good-to-excellent; northern Illinois farmers reporting excellent stands.
sections.bottom_line: Full reversal of Monday's break. Fund liquidation found the floor. Thursday exports resolve which bounce this is.
sections.vs_yesterday: Corn: Monday's window-closing call inverted; full reversal overnight.

sections.title: SOYBEANS AND MEAL FIRM
sections.body: Soybeans added less than 2 cents to $11.46, and soybean meal did the heavier lifting at $305.40, up 1.4%. Soybean oil gave back six-tenths of a percent to $66.52. Minnesota soybean conditions dropped from 80 to 76% good-to-excellent this week, a four-point slide that is sharper than the corn decline and not yet fully priced into the board. **Meal outperforming oil is a tell**: crush margins are being defended through feed demand, not through biofuel. With pollination windows approaching and soil moisture still running high in key districts, the weather story in beans is unresolved. The China $17 billion annual U.S. ag purchase commitment through 2028 remains the structural backdrop, but four-point weekly condition drops in Minnesota are the kind of news that puts a bid under meal faster than any diplomatic cable.
sections.catalyst: Minnesota soybean conditions dropped four points to 76% good-to-excellent in the latest USDA update.
sections.bottom_line: Meal leading soybeans higher says feed demand is the engine. Minnesota conditions are the watch.

sections.title: CATTLE FIRMS, DROUGHT CONTEXT MATTERS
sections.body: Live cattle closed $247.68, up four-tenths. Feeders ran to $371.07, up 1.1%, getting a lift directly from corn's overnight losses in Monday's session that carried into Tuesday's feeder pricing. The ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan and Schuyler plant lockout, now in its fifth week with 1,700 workers still out, continues to create processing-constrained dynamics rather than supply-constrained ones. But the number that reframes the whole cattle complex today is 53%: more than half of U.S. cattle inventory is sitting in drought-affected territory. **Drought plus lockout plus a herd that was already historically tight is a three-layer floor** under live prices. USDA's newly announced $60 million Small Processors Action Plan drew a welcome from the U.S. Cattlemen's Association, but $60 million spread across small processors does not replace 6,000 head-per-day of lost capacity at a single plant. Watch the cash market Thursday.
sections.catalyst: 53% of U.S. cattle inventory in drought-affected areas; USDA announces $60M Small Processors Action Plan.
sections.bottom_line: Drought covering 53% of inventory is the structural floor. Processing constraints are the ceiling problem.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: firmed modestly; drought scope adds structural support beyond the lockout story.

sections.title: ENERGY HOLDS, HORMUZ PREMIUM SHRINKS
sections.body: WTI crude held at $73.65, flat on the session. The Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, got a new chapter today: the U.S. issued a temporary two-month waiver allowing Iranian oil sales including in U.S. dollars until August 21, and Iran confirmed the U.S. agreed to unblock $12 billion in frozen Iranian funds. Indian Oil Corporation's tanker tender came up empty as Hormuz risk still keeps some commercial operators cautious, so the premium is shrinking but not gone. **Natural gas gave back 1.1% to $3.23**, sitting at just 14% of its 52-week range, a level that tells you the market sees no near-term demand catalyst from summer heat or industrial load. The Trump administration's note that Iran could buy U.S. ag products with unfrozen funds is the most interesting ag angle in the energy bucket today.
sections.catalyst: U.S. issues two-month Iranian oil sanctions waiver; $12 billion in frozen Iranian funds agreed to be unblocked.
sections.bottom_line: Hormuz premium deflating on real diplomatic movement. Iranian ag purchases a developing story worth tracking.

outside_the_pit.title: Senate Farm Bill Draft Drops Today from Boozman
outside_the_pit.body: Senate Agriculture Committee Chairman John Boozman releases the remaining farm bill sections Tuesday. With the One Big Beautiful Bill in the political background and nutrition program restructuring already announced by the Trump administration, this draft will show whether commodity title funding survived the budget pressure. Read the commodity title first.

outside_the_pit.title: New World Screwworm at 15 Cases, Criticism of USDA Response Builds
outside_the_pit.body: Three new screwworm cases were confirmed, bringing the total to 15, with 3 of those now inactive. Cattle producers in southern states are asking Agriculture Secretary Rollins for more transparency on the response timeline. With 53% of cattle inventory already in drought-affected areas, a screwworm spread northward would compound an already stressed supply picture.

outside_the_pit.title: Dairy Heifer Inventory Set to Shrink Before a 2027 Rebound
outside_the_pit.body: Financial incentives are steering more dairy calves into the beef supply rather than replacement heifer pools, according to Feedstuffs. A smaller heifer inventory means tighter dairy cow numbers heading into 2027, which puts a structural floor under Class III milk prices at a time when heat stress is already shaving yield and component quality. Dairy producers planning 2027 expansion should note the squeeze coming from both ends.

spread_to_watch.label: Corn nearby / Dec '26 spread
spread_to_watch.level: Flat at $0.00, no carry, no inverse
spread_to_watch.commentary: Nearby corn and Dec '26 both closed $4.41. A perfectly flat spread after a 6.8% overnight run in the nearby says the market is not yet pricing a sustained weather premium into new crop, it is pricing the same uncertainty across the board. If Minnesota conditions keep sliding and the spread flips to an inverse, that is the first sign new-crop supply risk is being taken seriously.

basis.headline: Corn basis mixed; northern Belt softening as conditions slip.
basis.body: Eastern Belt corn basis is holding after Monday's sharp break, with elevator bids reflecting the overnight recovery in futures. **Northern Belt basis is the watch**: as Minnesota and upper Midwest conditions show early weakness, merchandisers will start sorting between the strong-stand areas and the spotty ones. Western Belt basis remains soft, consistent with adequate nearby supply. Soybean basis is quiet, meal basis is firming on the back of today's meal strength.

weekly_thread.question: Does USDA Crop Progress confirm the Belt is ahead of pace, removing the last weather premium from corn?
weekly_thread.status_text: Monday's Crop Progress gave a split answer: northern Illinois looks excellent, but Minnesota corn and soybeans both dropped on the week. The weather premium wasn't removed. It relocated north. Corn's 6.8% overnight reversal says the market agrees.

the_more_you_know.title: When conditions slip 4 points in one week, what history says happens next.
the_more_you_know.body: Minnesota soybeans fell from 80 to 76% good-to-excellent in a single weekly report, a four-point slide. That pace matters because USDA research on final-yield relationships shows that sustained condition declines starting before pollination carry more weight than equivalent declines after silking. A four-point drop in late June is not a crisis, but it is the kind of early signal that, if it continues for two or three more weeks, will show up in August yield estimates. The market typically does not price this possibility until the second or third consecutive weekly decline. If next Monday's Crop Progress shows Minnesota soybeans at 72 or below, that is the number that forces a real conversation about new-crop supply. Today at 76%, you are watching the first data point on a potential trend, not reacting to a confirmed one.

watch_list.desc: Weekly export sales: corn above 400K MT confirms the overnight bounce has fundamental support. Below 300K MT, the run is fund positioning, not real demand.
USDA Crop Progress: Minnesota soybeans at 72% or below good-to-excellent is the trigger for a new-crop weather premium conversation.
Senate farm bill commodity title draft: watch commodity reference prices and ARC/PLC funding levels. Changes there move producer planting math for 2027.
Cash cattle trade: no clean price reset since the lockout started. The first negotiated cash trade above or below $248 sets the next leg.
U.S.-Iran sanctions waiver expires. If Iranian ag purchases begin before then, watch soybean export sales for unexpected demand. If talks stall, Hormuz risk premium rebuilds.

daily_quote.text: Rain does not fall on one roof alone.

source_summary: USDA Crop Progress (weekly), Brownfield Ag News, Beef Magazine, Feedstuffs, OilPrice.com, EIA, The Fence Post, CME Group settlement prices.
